always.exam: Observation & gait (Trendelenburg gait, antalgic pattern, leg length)
always.why: Baseline for every hip assessment; screens gluteal weakness gait pattern, antalgia, and limb-length asymmetry.
always.confirmsOrExcludes: Screens gait deviation and gross weakness pattern

always.exam: Active + passive hip ROM (flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, IR, ER) with end-feel and capsular pattern
always.why: Range and end-feel across all planes is required to identify a capsular (osteoarthritic) restriction pattern versus a soft-tissue-limited presentation.
always.confirmsOrExcludes: Screens capsular restriction pattern (IR most limited = early hip OA)

always.exam: Hip special test screen (FABER/Patrick's, FADIR, Scour)
always.why: First-line provocation cluster to broadly localise intra-articular (FAI/OA/labral) versus extra-articular (SIJ) pain sources.

add.exam: FADIR test + C-sign screen; consider imaging if impingement/labral tear suspected
add.why: FADIR-provoked anterior groin pain is the classic femoroacetabular impingement / labral tear pattern and warrants targeted confirmation.

add.exam: FADIR test + Hip Scour test (C-sign strongly suggests an intra-articular source)
add.why: The C-sign is a well-recognised marker of intra-articular hip pathology and should be corroborated with provocation testing.

add.exam: FABER/Patrick's test + SIJ provocation cluster to differentiate hip from SIJ source
add.why: FABER-provoked pain can arise from the hip joint (groin) or the SI joint (posterior pelvic pain) — the distribution during the test differentiates the two.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes hip joint vs SIJ source

add.exam: Resisted hip abduction (MMT gluteus medius/minimus) + Trendelenburg test + Ober's test
add.why: Lateral hip pain is the hallmark of greater trochanteric pain syndrome / gluteal tendinopathy; resisted abduction, Trendelenburg, and Ober's test together confirm or exclude it.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes greater trochanteric pain syndrome / gluteal tendinopathy

add.exam: Resisted hip extension / knee flexion (proximal hamstring) + palpation of ischial tuberosity
add.why: Pain localised to the ischial tuberosity, worse sitting, is the classic proximal hamstring tendinopathy pattern.

add.exam: Resisted hip adduction + adductor squeeze test + palpation of adductor origin/pubic ramus
add.why: Groin pain reproduced by resisted adduction localises to the adductor group and screens for athletic pubalgia/osteitis pubis.

add.exam: Palpate pubic symphysis + resisted adduction; screen for athletic pubalgia / osteitis pubis
add.why: Pubic symphysis-localised pain in an athletic or post-partum population needs a specific pubic-region screen distinct from simple adductor strain.

add.exam: Piriformis (FAIR) test + neurological screen to exclude a lumbar/sciatic source
add.why: Deep buttock pain with sciatic-type symptoms requires differentiating piriformis/deep gluteal syndrome from a true lumbar radiculopathy.

add.exam: Dynamic snapping hip test (active hip flexion/extension observing/palpating the snap)
add.why: Mechanical snapping needs to be reproduced and located (iliopsoas over the femoral head vs ITB over the trochanter) to confirm the snapping-hip subtype.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes internal vs external snapping hip syndrome

add.exam: Dynamic snapping hip test (active hip flexion/extension observing/palpating the snap)
add.why: Mechanical snapping needs to be reproduced and located (iliopsoas over the femoral head vs ITB over the trochanter) to confirm the snapping-hip subtype.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes internal vs external snapping hip syndrome

add.exam: Sensory testing over the lateral thigh (LFCN distribution) + Tinel's sign over the inguinal ligament
add.why: Lateral thigh burning/numbness without a lumbar source suggests meralgia paraesthetica (lateral femoral cutaneous nerve entrapment) rather than a musculoskeletal hip pathology.

supporting.exam: Neurological screen — hip flexion myotome (L1-L2) if red-flag or neurological concern
supporting.why: Rules out a proximal lumbar plexus/nerve root contribution when hip flexor weakness or atypical pain is present.
supporting.confirmsOrExcludes: Screens L1-L2 nerve root / femoral nerve contribution

supporting.exam: Functional + outcome measure (HOOS, Oxford Hip Score, iHOT-33, or HAGOS)
supporting.why: Quantifies baseline hip-related disability for goal-setting and re-assessment.

supporting.exam: Imaging (only if red flag, suspected fracture/AVN, or failed conservative management)
supporting.why: Imaging is not routine for mechanical hip/groin pain; reserve for red flags, suspected fracture/AVN, or a structural diagnosis that will change management.
supporting.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms structural pathology when clinically indicated
